item.title: TOURISM TALANOA | The rising tide of consultation
item.description: LAST week, the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Justice, Law and Human Rights convened a consultation on the Commercial Use of Marine Resources Bill 2025 (CUMA) through the Fiji Hotel & Tourism Association, commendably broadening tourism stakeholder input. It is uncommon for a parliamentary committee to directly engage a private sector association, despite many reforms significantly affecting t
item.content:encoded:
LAST week, the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Justice, Law and Human Rights convened a consultation on the Commercial Use of Marine Resources Bill 2025 (CUMA) through the Fiji Hotel & Tourism Association, commendably broadening tourism stakeholder input.

It is uncommon for a parliamentary committee to directly engage a private sector association, despite many reforms significantly affecting the industry when it is excluded.

This gesture warrants recognition, acknowledgement and some serious consideration.

Through FHTA, the industry has also been asked to contribute to several other reforms, including the Health Act, the Securities Act Review, the Liquor Act Review, the Yacht Charter Act, and the Fiji Ports Charges Review, among others. We have contributed to discussions, consultations and submissions on the Employment Relations Amendments Bill, the National Minimum Wage, the VAT Act Review, the VAT Monitoring System, the Tourism Act, the FNU Employer Levy, and make annual submissions on the National Budget.

Despite tourism’s broad economic impact, policymakers often overlook how legislative modernisation — designed primarily for public sector upgrades — often fails to keep pace with the industry’s rapid evolution. As a result, amendments frequently prove inconsequential or, worse, are misaligned with the diverse segments of this heavily relied-upon sector. Tourism has advanced in commercial strides, integrating global standards, digital transformation, and complex supply chains, yet reforms continue to lag.

This disconnect risks undermining competitiveness, creating regulatory friction, and eroding confidence in one of Fiji’s most critical economic drivers.

The recent raft of consultations on legislative reviews has therefore been welcomed, despite the one on taxes slipping through the cracks.

The CUMA Bill governs how businesses access and operate within Fiji’s marine areas, and for a significant share of our tourism industry, that access is not a peripheral consideration.

It is the foundation most operations are built on. A dive operation, a live-aboard charter, a marine-based excursion company, an island resort dependent on boat transfers and reef access – all of these operate at the direct intersection of commercial activity and marine tenure that this Bill is designed to regulate.

Given that reality, it has always been clear to us from the outset that this consultation must be as wide as possible given its scope. This legislation will apply regardless of status, and an issue of this magnitude warrants an industry-wide response rather than a narrower one shaped only around our own membership base and the turnout reflected that understanding and shared concern.

Operators from across the sector, large and small, attended and contributed, and the discussion was stronger for it.

What emerged from that session was a genuinely useful exchange. Committee members heard directly from people whose livelihoods depend on the outcome of this legislation, not through a written submission or a summarised position paper, but in person, in their own words. Some contributions were technical, addressing the practical mechanics of licensing, access arrangements and enforcement.

Others were more personal, reflecting the extent to which many of our operators have built their working lives, and in some cases their family histories, around a relationship with Fiji’s marine environment or relationships with their communities.

Both forms of input have value, and we hope the committee left that room with a clearer understanding of what this Bill means in practice, beyond its language on paper.

I want to address, with appropriate care, the sensitivity that sits underneath this entire conversation.

The Commercial Use of Marine Resources Bill does not exist in isolation. It intersects directly with questions of customary marine tenure and indigenous rights that long precede tourism as an industry in this country, and indeed long precede the modern Fijian state itself.

iTaukei communities hold legitimate and paramount rights over the resources within their qoliqoli, and any legislation touching commercial use of those waters must be built on that foundation as a starting principle, not as a qualification added afterwards.

This is not a matter of legal formality. It reflects a relationship with land and sea that runs deeper than commercial interest, and it deserves to be treated with the weight that history and custom give it.

This is not about catering to any single qoliqoli seeking to regain lost ground. It is about safeguarding a collective heritage and ensuring that legislation reflects the depth of customary rights and responsibilities.

Any framework for commercial use must honour that foundation, or risk undermining both indigenous authority and national cohesion.

Tourism’s engagement with this Bill is not, and should not be read as a challenge to that principle. What our industry brought to the table last week was a request to be included meaningfully in shaping how commercial access functions in practice, recognising that tourism is one of the sectors most materially affected by the eventual shape of this legislation.

We are not seeking preferential treatment, and we are conscious of how easily that impression could form if this issue is not communicated carefully. What we are seeking is genuine participation in a process that will determine how a significant part of our industry will operate for years to come.

Tourism remains the backbone of Fiji’s economy, supporting employment across every division of this country and generating the foreign exchange earnings that underpin much of our national output.

That is not asserted as leverage. It is simply the operating reality within which this legislation will be implemented, and it is precisely why we approach a matter this sensitive with restraint rather than assertion.

This brings me to the question that, in my view, matters more than the consultation session itself: what happens next?

Being consulted and being heard are not the same thing, and any organisation that has spent time engaging with government processes in this country will recognise the distinction. A consultation can shape an outcome meaningfully, or it can function as a procedural step that precedes a decision already largely settled.

I do not know at this stage which of those descriptions will eventually apply here. I hope, sincerely, that it is the former. But hope is not a substitute for evidence, and we will only know once we see how the concerns raised last week are reflected, or not reflected, in the Bill’s final form.

There is a comparison worth drawing here briefly, because it illustrates a pattern our industry has encountered before.

For some years now, the narrative around Fiji Airways and tourism has tended to run in one direction, with the airline and the Ministry of Tourism regularly emphasising that tourism depends on Fiji Airways.

That is true, and we do not dispute it. But it is an incomplete picture. Tourism, if required, retains the option of working with other carriers to bring visitors into this country.

item.title: CLEAR VISION FOR A BRIGHTER FUTURE | Eye health for an aging population
item.description: As people live longer, maintaining good health in later years has become more important than ever. Among the many aspects of healthy aging, eye health is often overlooked until vision problems begin to interfere with daily life. Good eyesight plays a vital role in maintaining independence, safety, and quality of life. With the global population of older adults increasing, protecting eye health mus
item.content:encoded:
As people live longer, maintaining good health in later years has become more important than ever. Among the many aspects of healthy aging, eye health is often overlooked until vision problems begin to interfere with daily life. Good eyesight plays a vital role in maintaining independence, safety, and quality of life. With the global population of older adults increasing, protecting eye health must become a priority for individuals, families, and communities.

Aging naturally brings changes to the eyes. Many people over the age of 40 notice difficulty reading small print, a condition known as presbyopia. While reading glasses can easily correct this problem, other age-related eye conditions can have a much greater impact on vision. Cataracts, glaucoma, age-related macular degeneration (AMD), and diabetic retinopathy are among the leading causes of visual impairment and blindness in older adults. The encouraging news is that many of these conditions can be treated or managed successfully if detected early.

Cataracts are one of the most common eye conditions affecting older people. They occur when the eye’s natural lens becomes cloudy, causing blurred vision, glare, and difficulty seeing at night. Cataracts usually develop gradually and may not initially affect daily activities. However, as they progress, they can significantly reduce vision. Fortunately, cataract surgery is one of the safest and most effective medical procedures available today, restoring clear vision for millions of people worldwide.

Glaucoma is often referred to as the “silent thief of sight” because it usually develops without noticeable symptoms in its early stages. The disease damages the optic nerve, often due to increased pressure inside the eye. Once vision is lost from glaucoma, it cannot be restored. However, early diagnosis through regular eye examinations and appropriate treatment with medications, laser therapy, or surgery can slow or prevent further vision loss.

Age-related macular degeneration affects the macula, the central part of the retina responsible for sharp, detailed vision. People with AMD may experience blurred central vision, making it difficult to read, drive, recognise faces, or perform close-up tasks. While there is currently no cure for advanced AMD, early detection, healthy lifestyle choices, and specific treatments for certain forms of the disease can help preserve vision for longer.

Diabetes also poses a serious threat to eye health. Diabetic retinopathy occurs when high blood sugar damages the tiny blood vessels in the retina. Many people may not notice symptoms until significant damage has occurred. Regular eye examinations, good blood sugar control, healthy blood pressure, and cholesterol management are essential to reducing the risk of vision loss among people living with diabetes.

Although aging itself cannot be prevented, many lifestyle choices can help maintain healthy eyes. A balanced diet rich in leafy green vegetables, colourful fruits, fish high in omega-3 fatty acids, nuts, and whole grains provide important nutrients that support eye health. Vitamins A, C, and E, along with zinc and lutein, have been associated with maintaining healthy vision.

Regular physical activity also benefits eye health by improving blood circulation and reducing the risk of conditions such as diabetes, high blood pressure, and heart disease, all of which can affect vision. Maintaining a healthy weight, avoiding smoking, and limiting excessive alcohol consumption further reduce the risk of developing serious eye diseases.

Protecting the eyes from harmful ultraviolet (UV) rays is another simple but effective preventive measure. Wearing sunglasses that block 100 per cent of UVA and UVB rays, along with a wide-brimmed hat when outdoors, can help reduce the long-term risk of cataracts and other eye damage caused by sun exposure.

Older adults should also take steps to reduce eye strain. Adequate lighting when reading, regular breaks from digital screens, and ensuring prescription glasses are up to date can improve comfort and vision. Simple home safety measures, such as good lighting, removing trip hazards, and using contrasting colours on stairs and steps, can also reduce the risk of falls caused by poor vision.

Perhaps the most important step in preserving eyesight is scheduling regular comprehensive eye examinations. Many serious eye diseases develop silently, without pain or obvious symptoms. Routine examinations allow eye care professionals to detect problems early, monitor changes over time, and begin treatment before permanent vision loss occurs. Adults over the age of 60, particularly those with diabetes, high blood pressure, a family history of eye disease, or previous eye injuries, should have regular eye check-ups as recommended by their eye care provider.

Families also play an important role in supporting the eye health of older relatives. Encouraging routine appointments, assisting with transportation to clinics, helping manage medications, and recognising early signs of vision changes can make a significant difference. Communities and healthcare providers can further support healthy aging by increasing public awareness about preventable blindness and improving access to affordable eye care services.

Healthy vision is essential for maintaining independence, mobility, and overall well-being throughout life. As our population continues to age, investing in eye health is an investment in healthy aging itself. Through regular eye examinations, healthy lifestyle choices, early treatment, and greater public awareness, many older adults can continue to enjoy clear vision and an active, fulfilling life for years to come. After all, preserving sight means preserving the ability to fully experience the world around us.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FCEF | Influencing labour policies
item.description: WORKPLACE health and safety is not only a regulatory responsibility – it is a business investment. Safe workplaces protect workers, strengthen businesses and contribute to a more productive and resilient economy. For employers, investing in workplace health and safety means more than meeting minimum requirements. It means creating systems, cultures and practices that support employees, reduce oper
item.content:encoded:
WORKPLACE health and safety is not only a regulatory responsibility – it is a business investment.

Safe workplaces protect workers, strengthen businesses and contribute to a more productive and resilient economy. For employers, investing in workplace health and safety means more than meeting minimum requirements. It means creating systems, cultures and practices that support employees, reduce operational risks and enable businesses to perform sustainably.

Poor safety practices can carry significant financial, operational and reputational consequences, particularly at a time when businesses are navigating rising costs, workforce pressures and increasing expectations around responsible business practices.

Businesses that invest in compliance, continuous training and education, quality equipment, effective risk management systems and a culture of wellness, safety and resilience are better positioned to improve productivity, retain employees and build long-term organisational strength.

According to the Ministry of Employment, Productivity and Workplace Relations (MEPWR):

(a) a total of 1040 notices were issued to businesses for breach of OHS. On average, this is about 367 a year;

(b) the top five infringement notices issued were: Improvement Notice (seven days to rectify), Requisition Notice (submission of documents), Penalties fines (no rectification done), Prohibition Notice (stop work order) and Rescinding Order (work may resume as issue rectified).

(c) the top five industries with the highest OHS infringements were Construction, Transport, Saw-milling, Mining and Quarry and Manufacturing.

MEPWR also recorded 1585 Workmen compensation claims pay out – valued at $16,899,850.00, between 2023 and March 2026. These figures highlight why workplace safety must remain a priority for employers, workers and policymakers. Beyond the financial cost, workplace incidents can impact families, employee wellbeing, productivity and business continuity. Effective workplace health and safety requires practical policies, clear guidance and ongoing collaboration between those who develop workplace frameworks and those responsible for implementing them.

This is where the Fiji Commerce & Employers Federation (FCEF) plays an important role.

As Fiji’s national employers’ organisation, FCEF advocates on behalf of businesses across a wide range of issues, including labour reform, workforce development, regulatory change and workplace health and safety.

One of the ways FCEF creates value for members is by ensuring that employer experiences help shape national policy discussions.

The National Occupational Safety and Health Advisory Board (NOSHAB) is one such platform.

Established under the Health and Safety at Work Act 1996, NOSHAB is a tripartite body bringing together representatives from Government, trade unions and employers to discuss occupational health and safety matters and provide advice on the continued development of workplace health and safety policy in Fiji. FCEF currently has five employer representatives serving on NOSHAB, ensuring that the experiences and perspectives of Fiji’s businesses are considered during national discussions on occupational health and safety. “The first NOSHAB meeting for this year is scheduled for August 13, 2026,” FCEF chief executive officer Edward Bernard said. “We have reached out to members for feedback on any challenges they are facing on OHS. This is the highest national policy body on OHS and therefore important to ensure that our representatives amplify the needs of our members and the private sector.”

Modernising Fiji’s workplace safety framework

Government has indicated that the Health and Safety at Work Act 1996, is up for review. After almost three decades, there is an opportunity to ensure Fiji’s workplace health and safety framework reflects the realities of modern workplaces, changing technologies and evolving business practices.

Work will start on it after the highly anticipated Employment Relations (Amendment) Bill 2025 and the Work Care Bill 2025 is finally passed by Parliament. This is a key legislation that is on FCEF’s workplan for the 2026-27 financial year. “The root cause of non-compliance by businesses can also be that policy makers in Government are not informed on what is working and what is not – perhaps the industry has transitioned to new systems and processes and our laws and inspectorate have not modernised. This is also why we want to hear from our members,” Mr Bernard said.

In the majority of workplaces in Fiji, business owners and managers are doing their part to ensure a healthy and safe work environment for staff, suppliers, customers and the public. In manufacturing and industrial operations, businesses have implemented risk assessments, machinery safeguards, employee training and improved reporting systems to manage workplace hazards.

In construction, employers continue to focus on areas such as working at heights, equipment safety, contractor management and site controls. In tourism and hospitality, businesses prioritise areas including food safety, emergency preparedness, guest safety and employee wellbeing. The recent National OHS Awards awarded FCEF members Vinod Patel and Fiji Airports as winners in their respective categories. Vinod Patel – Large Enterprise category. Fiji Airports – Fiji Government Subsidiary category. Both winners ranked high for their OHS governance, training & competency programmes, Risk management plan, employee engagement, and safety performance.

The COVID-19 pandemic provided one of the clearest examples of how workplace health and safety systems can protect workers while supporting economic continuity.

During this period, businesses across Fiji rapidly adapted their operations to introduce new workplace health measures, including hygiene protocols, employee health monitoring, revised operating procedures, emergency planning and new approaches to managing workplace risks. Across sectors, employers worked alongside Government agencies to implement measures that protected employees, customers and communities while allowing essential services and economic activity to continue. The experience demonstrated that when Government, employers and workers work together, practical workplace safety outcomes can be achieved at scale.

It also reinforced that businesses are not simply recipients of workplace safety policies but essential partners in implementing them.

The evolving meaning of workplace safety

Historically, OHS discussions have often centred around physical hazards—machinery, chemicals, workplace injuries and inspections. However, modern workplace health and safety increasingly recognises that employee wellbeing, mental health, fatigue management and preventative health are also important components of a safe and productive workplace. The revised Public Service Workplace Health and Wellness Policy (for Civil Service), for example, includes workplace health promotion, mental health, NCD prevention and initiatives such as Wellness Wednesdays as part of a broader healthy workplace approach. This reflects a growing recognition that employee wellbeing is closely linked to workforce productivity, engagement and resilience.

Many employers have also introduced their own wellness initiatives, recognising that supporting employees’ overall wellbeing contributes to stronger workplace culture, improved morale and greater workforce resilience.

While compliance is important, the reality is that one size does not fit all. Employers may encounter differing interpretations during inspections, difficulties implementing certain requirements, administrative challenges or emerging risks associated with new technologies and changing ways of working.

Smaller businesses may face additional challenges accessing specialised expertise, training resources or systems that larger organisations can more easily implement. Sharing both successes and challenges helps ensure occupational health and safety policy continues to evolve in a way that is practical, effective and responsive to Fiji’s diverse business community.

We are seeking feedback from businesses on occupational health and safety matters affecting their operations, including experiences relating to the Health and Safety at Work Act, workplace inspections, compliance requirements, policy implementation, administrative processes or emerging workplace risks. FCEF members are invited to submit their feedback through the FCEF Members Hub by 12pm on Friday, July 31, 2026, ahead of the upcoming NOSHAB meeting.

Sign in to Members Hub to share your feedback.

Vinod Patel won in the Large Enterprise Category of the 2026 OHS Awards held in Nadi in April. Picture: FCEF/SUPPLIED

item.title: LOCAL GETAWAYS | Appreciate the beauty of Fiji from the air
item.description: THERE is no better way to appreciate the beauty of Fiji than from the air. Step aboard a Sunflower Aviation Ltd scenic aeroplane flight and experience the breathtaking Mamanuca Islands from a whole new perspective.
item.content:encoded:
THERE is no better way to appreciate the beauty of Fiji than from the air.

Step aboard a Sunflower Aviation Ltd scenic aeroplane flight and experience the breathtaking Mamanuca Islands from a whole new perspective.

From the moment you take off from Nadi International Airport, you’ll be treated to uninterrupted panoramic views of Fiji’s world-renowned turquoise waters, pristine white-sand beaches, vibrant coral reefs, and lush tropical islands.

Whether you’re planning a memorable family outing, celebrating a special occasion, entertaining visiting friends and relatives, or simply ticking another experience off your Fiji bucket list, our 35-minute Mamanuca Scenic Flight promises an unforgettable journey through one of the most picturesque island groups in the South Pacific.

As you soar above the islands, you’ll witness the incredible colours of Fiji’s lagoons, secluded beaches, luxury island resorts, and the stunning reef systems that make our islands famous around the world.

Don’t forget your camera, this is a view you’ll want to capture and remember forever.

Our local resident rate for four passenger flight is around $460 per person, while our six passenger flight is around $320 per person.

Whether you’re flying with family, friends, or colleagues, our scenic flights offer a unique opportunity to create lasting memories while discovering Fiji from an entirely new perspective.

Flight Details, departure location, Nadi International Airport – Domestic Terminal, Counter 5.

Operating Hours, seven days a week, scenic Flights: 8am – 5pm.

At Sunflower Aviation, safety, comfort, and exceptional customer service are at the heart of every flight.

Our experienced pilots are passionate about showcasing the spectacular beauty of Fiji, ensuring every passenger enjoys a smooth, safe, and memorable experience from take-off to landing.

Whether you’re a first-time flyer or simply looking to fall in love with Fiji all over again, a Sunflower Aviation scenic flight is an experience not to be missed.

Seats are limited, so book your scenic adventure today.

For bookings and enquiries, Email: bookings@flyinfiji.com, Phone: +679 9924940

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: POINT OF ORIGIN | Give ‘half a chance’ | Society targets equality, full participation
item.description: FOR most people, catching a bus, crossing a busy road, reading a sign or walking into a government office are everyday tasks that barely require a second thought. For a person who is blind or partially sighted, those same routines can become daily tests of courage, independence and determination.
item.content:encoded:
FOR most people, catching a bus, crossing a busy road, reading a sign or walking into a government office are everyday tasks that barely require a second thought.

For a person who is blind or partially sighted, those same routines can become daily tests of courage, independence and determination.

That is the reality the Fiji Society for the Blind (FSB) has been working to change for the past 56 years.

Established through the vision of the late social worker Frank Hilton, the late Dr Asad Ali Asgar and members of the Rotary Club of Suva, the organisation has grown into Fiji’s leading centre for services supporting blind and visually impaired people.

Today, it provides education, rehabilitation, orientation and mobility training, Braille transcription, early intervention, hostel accommodation, community-based rehabilitation and primary eye care — all free of charge.

Its mission is simple but ambitious: to ensure blind and visually impaired people enjoy equality with sighted people and full participation in society.

Executive director Barbara Farouk said that mission had guided every step of the organisation’s journey.

“I am very privileged to be serving the Fiji Society for the Blind as executive director,” she said.

“Twenty-six years ago, when I joined the organisation, apart from an accounting qualification and some leadership experience, I had little or no knowledge of the needs and aspirations of blind and visually impaired persons.”

She said leading the organisation had been one of the greatest privileges of her life.

“I thank the Lord Almighty for giving me the courage to take up the challenge to hold the reins of this august organisation and lead it to greater heights.

“I also wish to thank the former and current board members for having faith in me and guiding and supporting me to steer this very special organisation all these years.”

But Ms Farouk believes understanding blindness begins with a simple exercise.

“Close your eyes and welcome to the land of the blind,” she said.

“Are you different? Has your mind, your hands, legs stopped working?

Any difference in your goals, love for family and friends, desires, anxieties, appearance? No. You have the same love, worries, desires and mind chatter.

“On a higher plane, there’s nothing wrong with you. You see things differently. You have greater challenges but the desire to improve is still there.”

She then asks people to imagine navigating a world that was never designed with blindness in mind.

“Now, still with your eyes closed, consider going home today via public transport without being trained by FSB.

“Imagine going to a public high school for the first time, crossing the road or walking on uneven pavements, going to shops or government departments without any signs in Braille, visiting hospitals with no Braille signage or trying to find a toilet in a new place.

“Imagine being blind or partially sighted and studying for exams without Braille papers, doing external and tertiary examinations, facing the added stress and still not having longer to complete the papers.

“Now imagine that your child cannot see.”

Those experiences, she said, explain why the society was established more than five decades ago.

“This is why FSB was established 56 years ago by the late Frank Hilton, a group of social workers, the late Dr Asad Ali Asgar and the Rotary Club of Suva, which provided the funding.”

The organisation started modestly after the Government provided land for its operations. Three wooden buildings, originally used during the construction of the Suva-Nadi highway, became its first home.

Ms Farouk said over the years, support from donors transformed the campus.

“Sixteen years later these were updated thanks to donors such as Christoffel Blinden Mission, Rotary Club of Suva, The Fiji Times through the Fiji Sixes Charity and the Bailey Trust.”

She added that the Narseys Plastics family funded the hostels, while Mr and Mrs Hari Punja financed the construction of the administration block.

Despite the progress, she said the society’s vision remains unfinished.

“Our goal is to reach equality with sighted persons and full participation in society through education, rehabilitation, social integration, employment and legislation.

item.title: FOCUS I Growth alone won’t keep workers home
item.description: ECONOMIC growth has long been regarded as the ultimate measure of a nation’s success. Rising gross domestic product (GDP), stronger tourism earnings, increased investment and expanding exports have traditionally been viewed as signs of a healthy economy. But according to International Labour Organization (ILO) Head of Education and Training Dr Naren Prasad, Fiji has reached a point where these tra
item.content:encoded:
ECONOMIC growth has long been regarded as the ultimate measure of a nation’s success. Rising gross domestic product (GDP), stronger tourism earnings, increased investment and expanding exports have traditionally been viewed as signs of a healthy economy.

But according to International Labour Organization (ILO) Head of Education and Training Dr Naren Prasad, Fiji has reached a point where these traditional indicators no longer tell the full story.

While delivering his remarks at a public lecture at the University of the South Pacific this week, the Fijian economist argued that the country’s next development challenge is no longer simply growing the economy, but ensuring that growth creates productive jobs, retains skilled workers and improves the lives of ordinary people.

His central message hovered on the central message that Fiji needs to move from a “growth-first” model to a “jobs-first” economy.

Dr Prasad believes Fiji’s labour market has changed more rapidly than its institutions, education system and policy responses.

Thirty years ago, unemployment dominated economic discussions. Today, businesses across multiple sectors report they cannot recruit enough workers despite thousands of young people entering the workforce every year.

Construction firms struggle to find tradespeople. Hospitals cannot retain nurses. Schools are short of teachers. Hotels compete aggressively for staff, while employers increasingly recruit workers from overseas.

At the same time, many young graduates continue to search for meaningful employment that matches their qualifications.

This apparent contradiction is, according to Dr Prasad, evidence that Fiji’s labour market has entered a fundamentally different phase of development rather than facing isolated problems.

One of the strongest parts of Dr Prasad’s lecture was his description of what he called Fiji’s “six labour market paradoxes”.

Although the economy continues to expand, employers increasingly report labour shortages. Economic growth is creating demand for workers faster than the country is producing the skills businesses require.

Fiji has never produced so many school leavers, university graduates and Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) graduates, yet employers continue reporting widespread skills shortages.

The issue, he argues, is no longer educational access but educational relevance.

Employers need digital capability, technical trades, artificial intelligence skills, green technologies, communication skills and problem-solving abilities that are not always aligned with existing training pathways.

The third paradox concerns employment itself.

While unemployment remains a concern for many young people, thousands of vacancies remain unfilled because employers cannot find workers with the required skills.

This highlights the growing mismatch between education outputs and labour market demand.

Perhaps Fiji’s biggest economic challenge today is migration.

Thousands of skilled workers—including nurses, teachers, electricians, engineers and hospitality professionals—continue to leave for Australia, New Zealand and other overseas destinations.

Migration itself is not necessarily negative.

Dr Prasad acknowledged it brings remittances, international experience and global professional networks.

However, the growing outflow of skilled workers has created shortages across critical sectors.

Ironically, while Fiji exports skilled labour, employers are increasingly importing foreign workers to fill vacancies.

Rather than attempting to stop migration, he argues Fiji should focus on becoming a country where talented people want to stay—or eventually return.

Competitive wages, professional development, affordable housing, quality education and stronger public services all influence where skilled people choose to build their futures.

Another major issue highlighted during the lecture was women’s participation in the workforce.

Despite women being more educated than ever before, female labour force participation remains around 36 per cent compared with about 66 per cent for men.

At a time when employers complain about labour shortages, Dr Prasad believes Fiji is overlooking one of its greatest economic resources.

Policies such as affordable childcare, flexible working arrangements, safer transport and equal career opportunities could substantially increase workforce participation while boosting national productivity.

Rather than viewing gender equality solely as a social issue, he argued it should also be regarded as an economic strategy.

item.title: HISTORY I Charlie and the rise of Bau
item.description: DEEP within himself, the Swede Charles Savage —the descendant of the Vikings—felt a quiet satisfaction as he watched the Bau warriors who had been glaring angrily at him gradually avert their eyes. He did not look only around those aboard the sacred canoe. He also cast his gaze towards the occupants of all the other canoes, yet not a single man dared meet his eyes.
item.content:encoded:
DEEP within himself, the Swede Charles Savage —the descendant of the Vikings—felt a quiet satisfaction as he watched the Bau warriors who had been glaring angrily at him gradually avert their eyes.

He did not look only around those aboard the sacred canoe. He also cast his gaze towards the occupants of all the other canoes, yet not a single man dared meet his eyes.

“Let them boast as much as they wish. Let them tell everyone how many men they killed. Had it not been for my help, they would all have died at Nakelo, or at best have fled back to Bau in defeat.”

Once again Savage reflected that, so long as the Vunivalu continued to recognise him and appreciate the value of his firearm, what—or who—did he really have to fear?

While these thoughts occupied his mind and the Bau warriors remained silent, the fleet entered the river mouth before setting course for Bau.

The islands gradually came into view, and with them came the sound of celebration.

The sight of their homeland delighted the warriors, for this was the first occasion on which they could celebrate a victory over a territory belonging to Rewa.

Conversation soon resumed, but it was no longer about boasting as it had been earlier.

Now they spoke only of the festivities that awaited them: the women who would welcome them home, the victory dances that would be performed in honour of the returning warriors, the great feasts that would be prepared, and the days of celebration and rejoicing that would follow.

Stories of individual exploits in battle would be told later, separately, within each clan gathering.

When Savage observed the happiness of the Bau warriors and saw that the expressions of jealousy and suspicion had disappeared from their faces, a feeling of loneliness suddenly overcame him.

He realised that once the celebrations began, he too would once again be forgotten.

When they reached Bau, there would be no one to greet him or recognise him, for he was merely a stranger with neither family nor relatives there.

Then, quite suddenly, his face brightened as he remembered that he did in fact have someone in Bau whom he regarded as family—Lui, the Chinese cook from the ship Elisa.

Savage had almost forgotten Lui amidst the many demands of the previous weeks, after sailing first to Nairai with Daulakeba and then onward to Oneata in search of the new weapon—the European firearm.

Yet ever since the foreign ship had arrived at Nairai, Lui had remained constantly at Savage’s side, looking up to him as his protector and guardian among these formidable people, the Fijians.

During their time in Bau they had become especially close.

As he thought of Lui, Savage said to himself:

“I have been a useless protector, for I have scarcely thought about Lui despite his loyalty to me. When I return to Bau, I shall make every effort to strengthen our friendship, for in this place he truly is my only relative.”

Thus, as they entered Bau amid loud cheers of triumph, the beating of the lali drums and preparations for the victory celebrations, Savage clearly saw how little notice was taken of him.

The people of Bau were greeting only those whom they knew personally, embracing relatives and welcoming one another home.

Savage immediately began searching the crowd for Lui.

He examined every face, looking for the short, dark-complexioned Chinese man.

When they finally disembarked, Savage went straight to the Vunivalu and asked where Lui was.

Ratu Naulivou turned to one of the chiefs who had remained behind to guard the settlement and asked him about the Chinese man.

The chief replied that Lui had been sent, together with several others, to work in the plantations at Tai ko Bau.

A surge of anger came over Savage, and he could no longer restrain himself. He spoke firmly to Ratu Naulivou, insisting that Lui be summoned back immediately.

Because the Vunivalu was in such high spirits after the victory, he did not take offence at Savage’s forceful request. Instead, he immediately gave orders for Savage’s companion, Lui, to be brought back.

The entire island and the settlement buzzed with activity as the Bau warriors dispersed to reunite with their own families.

Money was brought out, ovens were prepared, and everywhere people hurried about making arrangements.

item.title: UNITY I Fiji Muslims, reconciliation and the shared future of Fiji
item.description: THE establishment of Fiji’s Truth and Reconciliation Commission marks an important moment in the country’s democratic journey. It provides an opportunity not only to examine the causes and consequences of political upheaval, but also to strengthen the foundations of a society built on justice, inclusion and mutual respect.
item.content:encoded:
THE establishment of Fiji’s Truth and Reconciliation Commission marks an important moment in the country’s democratic journey.

It provides an opportunity not only to examine the causes and consequences of political upheaval, but also to strengthen the foundations of a society built on justice, inclusion and mutual respect.

Truth and reconciliation cannot succeed by documenting events alone. They require communities to reflect honestly on their experiences, acknowledge the suffering of others and commit themselves to a shared future founded on equal citizenship and human dignity.

The Fiji Muslim community welcomes the opportunity to contribute to this national conversation. For more than one hundred and forty-five years Muslims have regarded Fiji as their permanent home.

Throughout this period, they have lived peacefully alongside indigenous iTaukei, Indo-Fijians and other communities, contributing to the country’s educational, social and economic development while maintaining cordial relations across religious and cultural boundaries.

There is no history of organised communal violence involving Fiji’s Muslims. Instead, the community has consistently sought to build bridges of understanding through education, service and engagement with the wider society.

This submission reflects on the experiences of Muslims during Fiji’s periods of political instability and considers how those experiences can contribute to a broader national dialogue on reconciliation. It also outlines principles that may assist Fiji in building a more cohesive, democratic and inclusive society.

The coups of 1987, the civil unrest surrounding the events of 2000 and the political changes of 2006 affected every sector of Fijian society. Although each event arose under different political circumstances, together they created an enduring sense of uncertainty that altered the social landscape of the nation.

Muslims, like many other communities, experienced these changes not only as political events but as deeply personal disruptions to family life, community institutions and individual aspirations.

Perhaps the most significant consequence was the loss of confidence in Fiji’s long-term stability. Many families who had invested generations in building their lives here concluded that their future was uncertain.

Migration became both a practical response and an emotional decision. Parents sought greater security for their children, professionals accepted opportunities overseas and entire family networks gradually relocated. This movement represented far more than demographic change. It weakened community institutions, disrupted social networks and reduced the pool of experienced leadership available to guide younger generations.

Muslim schools, religious organisations and voluntary associations were particularly affected. Experienced teachers, administrators, community leaders and professionals who had spent decades building these institutions departed for new lives abroad.

Their absence created leadership gaps that required years of rebuilding. Community organisations adapted by developing new leaders and strengthening volunteerism, yet the loss of accumulated experience remained significant.

Economic consequences accompanied these social changes. Farms, businesses and professional practices were abandoned or transferred, while investment decisions increasingly reflected concerns about political uncertainty.

Families that remained in Fiji often found themselves caring for elderly relatives whose children had emigrated. The emotional burden of separation became an often overlooked consequence of migration, affecting family cohesion across generations.

Although the actions of a minority did not reflect the views of the wider population, the events created doubts that persisted well beyond the immediate crisis. For some, those experiences permanently altered their sense of belonging.

Yet time also revealed the resilience of Fiji’s communities. A generation has now grown to adulthood with little direct memory of the events of 1987.

While they recognise the trauma experienced by their parents and grandparents, they are generally more focused on building their own future than reliving the divisions of the past.

The Muslim understanding of reconciliation is grounded in principles that have guided Islamic civilisation since its earliest years. The example most often cited is the establishment of the community in Medina under the leadership of Prophet Muhammad.

Faced with a society divided by tribal conflict and religious diversity, he sought not dominance but coexistence. Different communities retained their own beliefs and traditions while agreeing to principles of justice, mutual responsibility and peaceful coexistence.

This historical experience remains relevant because it demonstrates that lasting peace depends upon institutions that protect diversity while fostering shared citizenship. Social harmony cannot be achieved by erasing differences; it is sustained by recognising diversity as a permanent feature of human society and establishing fair relationships among all communities.

These principles have shaped the experience of Muslims in Fiji. From the earliest settlements, Muslim families established mosques, schools and community organisations that nurtured religious identity while encouraging positive engagement with the wider society.

Fiji was never viewed as a temporary place of residence but as home. Consequently, community development focused not only on preserving faith but also on contributing to the common good of the nation.

Islam teaches that humanity shares a common origin and that diversity among peoples is intended to encourage understanding rather than conflict. Different languages, cultures and traditions are therefore not obstacles to national unity but opportunities for mutual learning. This understanding complements democratic values that recognise the equal dignity of every citizen regardless of ethnicity, religion or cultural background.

Social cohesion is not created through legislation alone. It grows gradually through families, neighbourhoods, schools, places of worship and community organisations. These institutions cultivate the habits of trust, cooperation and shared responsibility that enable diverse societies to flourish.

For Fiji’s Muslims, the family remains the primary institution where these values are learned. Respect for parents, care for elders, responsibility towards children and concern for neighbours form the ethical foundation upon which wider civic responsibility develops. Religious education reinforces these values by emphasising honesty, compassion, justice and service to humanity.

Mosques have traditionally served not only as places of worship but also as centres of education, welfare and community dialogue. Through sermons, classes and public engagement, they have encouraged peaceful coexistence, respect for lawful authority and constructive participation in national life. Religious instruction has consistently emphasised that devotion to God is inseparable from responsibility towards fellow citizens.

Education has played an equally important role in strengthening social cohesion. Muslim schools have operated within Fiji’s national education system while welcoming students from all religious and ethnic backgrounds. Their contribution extends beyond academic achievement. They have provided environments where young people learn to live together peacefully.

Generations of graduates from these schools now serve Fiji as teachers, doctors, engineers, public servants, business leaders, farmers and skilled workers, contributing to every sector of national life.

These investments illustrate an important lesson for reconciliation. Peace is not sustained solely through constitutional arrangements or political settlements. It depends equally upon strong social institutions that cultivate trust, responsibility and shared purpose across generations.
